coredata.dc:description: We report the synthesis of yellow luminescent carbon quantum dots (CDQs; size: 2–9 nm) whose surface was functionalized with polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000) using a novel low-pressure microwave solvothermal method (MWS) that yielded highly emitting CQDs with quantum yields up to 61.1 % at λem =580 nm. The resulting quantum dots were characterized by 500-MHz 1H- and 13C-NMR, by FT-IR, UV–vis and photoluminescence spectroscopies, by XRD and XPS, by TEM microscopy, and by dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques. A comparison between the MWS method and the conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) method demonstrated significant advantages of the novel MWS methodology, which can find further applications in studies of state-of-the-art syntheses of CQDs, in particular, and potentially other quantum dots, in general.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the generation of photocarriers upon illumination with UV/Vis light at the absorption edge of the CQDs or directly into the surface states on illumination at different wavelengths and subsequent relaxation of the photocarrier electron into the surface traps (surface states; SSn). Differently colored photoluminescence can then occur by recombination of the photocarriers.

Proposed reaction scheme of solvent-engineered photoluminescent CQDs synthesized with a conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) method (DMSO: dimethyl sulfoxide, MeOH: methanol, EtOH: ethanol and EG: ethylene glycol).

(a) Photographs of carbon products under non-UV and UV (365 nm) irradiation in Ac2O (acetone), MeOH (methanol), Hex (hexane), EtOH (ethanol), H2O (water), DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide), EG (ethylene glycol) and NMP (N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone); quantum yields (Φ) are also given. (b) Time-dependent decay of the photoluminescence of CQDs synthesized in the absence of PEG by the MWS method (red empty circles) and by the conventional CAS method (blue empty triangles); note that no decay of the photoluminescence occurred in the presence of PEG-6000 (0.60 g) to the MWS (red full circles) and the CAS method (blue full triangles). (c) Plots showing the changes in the observed quantum yields with the amount of added PEG-6000 from CQDs synthesized by the MWS (red full circles) and CAS (blue full triangles) methods. For more details on the absorption spectra and photoluminescence spectra, see Figs. S1 and S2 (Supplementary Information).

Photographs displaying the dispersity of CQDs under non-UV and UV (365 nm) irradiation in H2O (water), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), 2-propanol (2-PrOH), 1-butanol (BuOH), acetone (Ac2O), and hexane (Hex).

(a) 1H NMR sepctra in D2O solvent, (b) 13C NMR spectra in D2O solvent, (c) FT-IR and (d) XRD spectra of CQDs synthesized by the microwave-assisted solvothermal method (red) or by the conventional (blue) solvothermal method (i) without the addition of PEG and (ii) with the addition of 0.60 g of PEG-6000. Black-colored spectra represents those of PEG-6000. (e, f) High-resolution XPS spectra of CQDs synthesized in the presence of added 0.60 g of PEG-6000 following (e) the MWS and (f) CAS treatments {(i) C 1 s; (ii) O 1 s}.

TEM images of CQDs synthesized (a) in the presence of 0.60 g of PEG-6000 and (b) in the absence of PEG by (i) the microwave solvothermal (MWS) and (ii) the conventional autoclaved solvothermal (CAS) methods, respectively, and (iii) DLS spectra of synthesized CQDs (a) with 0.60 g of PEG 6000 and (b) without PEG added. Red and blue bars represent the size distribution of MWS and CAS synthesized CQDs, respectively.

(a) Microwave power input and (b) temperatures measured during the reaction: black and blue lines represent the reaction conditions with and without the addition of the carbon precursor, respectively; (c) relative dielectric loss of ethylene glycol (black line with empty circles), polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000), and NaOH mixed ethylene glycol solution (no contamination of carbon precursor; black line with empty triangles) and the mixed solution used under experimental condition (contamination of a carbon precursor; red line with empty squares). (d) Heating time-dependent color transition of the solution containing 9,10-dinitroantheracene, NaOH, ethylene glycol solvent, and PEG-6000 (0.60 g); photographs were taken with an inner camera attached to the microwave apparatus; numbers above the photographs show the heating times. (e) TEM image of the carbon-based product after heating for 60 min.

Schematic illustration of the proposed mechanistic stages in the synthesis of CQDs with coordination to PEG: (a) polymerization, (b) pyrolysis and PEG-functionalization of the surface of CQDs by the MWS method, and (c) polymerization of CQDs by the CAS method from the viewpoint of obvious morphological differences between MWS- and CAS-synthesized CQDs (see inserted TEM images).
Relationships between the amount of polyethylene glycol (PEG) and quantum yields (Φs) of the synthesized CQDs by the MWS and CAS methods.

head.ce:title: Luminescent monodispersed carbon quantum dots by a microwave solvothermal method toward bioimaging applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report the synthesis of yellow luminescent carbon quantum dots (CDQs; size: 2–9 nm) whose surface was functionalized with polyethylene glycol (PEG-6000) using a novel low-pressure microwave solvothermal method (MWS) that yielded highly emitting CQDs with quantum yields up to 61.1 % at λem =580 nm. The resulting quantum dots were characterized by 500-MHz 1H- and 13C-NMR, by FT-IR, UV–vis and photoluminescence spectroscopies, by XRD and XPS, by TEM microscopy, and by dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques. A comparison between the MWS method and the conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) method demonstrated significant advantages of the novel MWS methodology, which can find further applications in studies of state-of-the-art syntheses of CQDs, in particular, and potentially other quantum dots, in general.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanostructured materials bridge the gap between bulk systems and molecular level systems, and lead to entirely new venues for applications, especially in chemical catalysis, electronics, optoelectronics and biology, among others [1]. Solids that exhibit distinct variations of optical and electronic properties with differences in particle sizes less than 100 nm are typically referred to as nanostructured materials that are categorized as either two-dimensional (e.g. thin films or quantum wells), one-dimensional (e.g. nanowires or quantum wires), or zero-dimensional (e.g., nanoparticles or quantum dots).

The last two decades have witnessed much enthusiasm directed at the optoelectronic properties of nanostructured semiconductors and quantum dots (QDs), as many fundamental properties are size-dependent (within the nanometer range) [2]. Several studies on QDs have focused on semiconductor nanomaterials involving heavy metals and rare metals which, in many instances, have not found their way to industrial utilization. Consequently, conversion from metal-based to carbon-based nanomaterials has been an important challenge with regard to abundant resources, eco-friendliness, and high functionalities. Carbon-based materials can perform better than metals and semiconductors with regard to structure. For instance, the carbon-based buckyball (i.e., buckminsterfullerene) was reported for the first time in 1984 by Rohlfing and coworkers [3] who used a laser to vaporize carbon in a supersonic helium beam. As newcomers in the class of carbonaceous nanomaterials, luminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs; size < 10 nm) have attracted inordinate attention because of their valuable properties (viz. eco-friendliness, and photoluminescence and electroluminescence in the blue to green spectral range: 435–560 nm) [4]. The properties of these CQDs adapt well in such further applications as bioimaging [5], heavy metal-ion detection [6], and as carbon Q-LED light sources [7]. Accordingly, there are strong reasons to switch from existing metal or semiconductor quantum dots to carbon-based quantum dots.

To date, various phenomenological protocols have been used to synthesize these carbon quantum dots: solvothermal treatment [6], carbonization [8], ultrasounds [9], and microwave chemistry [10] using such carbon precursors as juice [11], fruit peels [12], vegetables [13], paper [10], organic acids and bases [9,10,14,15], and aromatic compounds [7]. Unlike existing metallic and semiconductor quantum dots, however, CQDs display some undesirable features as well, which have impeded their widespread use in industry because of their syntheses that typically necessitate extremely harsh conditions as occur, for instance, in the conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) method. This methodology typically yields CQDs that display low photoluminescence quantum yields (Φ). For instance, earlier synthetic methods yielded CQDs with very low luminescence quantum yields in the spectral region λ = 580–700 nm, although some improvement has recently been reported by Yuan and coworkers [7] who obtained highly luminescent, multicolored CQDs (emitting from blue to red with a Φ up to 54–72 %) by a solvothermal treatment or refluxing of phloroglucinol in ethanol, followed by heating in an autoclave at 200 °C for 9 and 24 h. Likewise, Wang et al. [16] obtained red emitting CQDs (Φ = 53 %) synthesized from 1,3-dihydroxynaphthalene and KIO4 in ethanol heated in an autoclave at 180 °C for 1 h. From their part, Liu and coworkers [17] obtained highly blue-emitting N-doped CQDs (Φ up to 94.5 %; λem =400 nm) synthesized in natural water through a hydrothermal method with folic acid as the single precursor heated in an oven for 6 h at 240 °C for carbonization. It is evident that in many of these synthetic methods, severe reaction conditions (involving precursors with over 30 atoms, and heating for 5–24 h) were more the rule than the exception [10]. Consequently, a novel solvothermal treatment that is energy-efficient is desirable, even though energy-efficient synthetic protocols could, in principle, still produce CQDs with low emission quantum yields.

To the best of our knowledge, yellow-red emitting CQDs synthesized from biomass sources have been rather limited because of their somewhat weak photoluminescence. For instance, a rapid and energy-efficient microwave-assisted reaction catalyzed by a solid catalyst involving a lignocellulosic residue from the pulp and paper industry (primary sludge) resulted in weakly luminescent CQDs in the 350–550 nm spectral region [10]. Likewise, Liu et al. [18] obtained weakly blue-emitting CQDs (Φ = 22 %) from a one-step microwave heating method using citric acid, l-cysteine and dextrin as the precursors for the purpose of monitoring the presence of Cu2+ in environmental water samples, while Xu and coworkers [19] used a microwave-assisted rapid synthesis to obtain amphibious yellow fluorescent carbon dots (Φ ∼ 8 %) from glucose and phosphoric acid in aqueous media that proved useful as colorimetric nanosensors to discriminate between Cr(III) and Cr(VI). Despite the latter progress, however, an increase in the photoluminescence ability of eco-friendly synthesized CQDs remains a topic of considerable interest.

Though microwaves have been used to synthesize various nanomaterials [20], their effects in syntheses have remained highly unexplored and their use under solvothermal conditions has been rather limited. To overcome some of the impediments on the applications of CQDs (e.g., for bioimaging purposes), we herein report optimized conditions of a novel synthesis of CQDs with anthracene as the initial precursor followed by 9,10-dinitroanthracene and subsequently by 9,10-dihydroxyanthracene as the ultimate precursor that involves a low-pressure, energy-efficient, microwave-assisted solvothermal method. This method yielded yellowish-red emitting CQDs with significantly enhanced emission quantum yields (Φ > 60 %). Solvent effects were also investigated in the optimization process (methanol, ethanol, 2-propanol, butanol, acetone, hexane, and ethylene glycol) with respect to pressure conditions and in the selection of capping agents (PEG-1500 and PEG-6000) to functionalize the surface of the CQDs. We also report a comparative study between the novel microwave solvothermal method (MWS) and the more conventional autoclave solvothermal method (CAS) to explore the possible advantages of the MWS protocol in the synthesis of highly luminescent CQDs. In addition, the present study reports, for the first time, GO-structured CQDs from a bottom-up synthetic approach using the MWS treatment; graphene oxide (GO) quantum dots are known as members of the BCNO-type of quantum dots [21].

The following reagents were used as received from Fujifilm-Wako Pure Chem. Ind. Ltd.: anthracene (010−04222), aqueous nitric acid solution (HNO3; 010−04222, 143−01326), sodium hydroxide (198–13765), ethylene glycol (EG; 058−00986), PEG-600 (168−09075; mean molecular weight, 600), PEG-1500 (162−09095; mean molecular weight, 1500), and PEG-6000 (169−09125; mean molecular weight, 6000). Ion-exchanged water was utilized in the purification and characterization stages. The various solvents used (methanol, ethanol, 2-propanol, butanol, acetone, and hexane) were also purchased from Fujifilm-Wako Pure Chemical Ind. Ltd., as were the CDCl3 (039–25191) and D2O (041–34241) solvent for the 1H NMR and 13CNMR analyses.

Briefly, the white-colored solid anthracene (0.143 g, 0.80 mmol) was added to an aqueous solution of HNO3 (20 mL) in a three-necked 50-mL round bottom flask fitted with a condenser, after which the mixture was refluxed at 80 °C for 4 h using oil-bath heating. The mixture was subsequently cooled to ambient temperature and then centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min. Following filtration with excess ion-exchange water, the resultant yellow 9,10-dinitroanthracene was obtained by drying the precipitate at 60 ℃ in an electric furnace; its purity was determined by 1H-NMR techniques.

Ethylene glycol (20 mL) and 9,10-dinitroanthracene (0.4 mmol) were added to a reactor vial containing one of the solvents, following which the mixture was ultrasonicated at 20 kHz for 10–15 min to yield a yellow-colored solution. The latter was then transferred to a reaction glass tube (30 mL) for the microwave solvothermal treatment (MWS), or to a Teflon-lined stainless steel tube for the conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) treatment. Functionalization of the CQDs was then examined for variant polymerization degrees/amounts of PEG by addition of the three different PEGs (PEG-600, PEG-1500, or PEG-6000) and NaOH (6.0 mmol) to the yellow solution, which was rapidly (120 °C min–1) or slowly (1 °C min–1) heated to 210 °C using the MWS method and then kept at 210 °C for 3 h. For comparison purposes, the conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) method was also used in the synthesis of CQDs using an electric furnace at an optimal heating rate of 1 °C min–1 for a total heating time of 5 h. Subsequent rapid cooling to room temperature produced a dark-brown CQD solution. Removal of impurities from the CQD solution was achieved by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 5 min with hexane as the solvent and 30 min with methanol as the solvent; this procedure was repeated at least twice, following which the nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol that resulted in a yellow luminescent purified CQD solution.

The overall microwave single-mode system consisted of a Monowave 400 (Anton Parr Co. Ltd.; frequency, 2.45 GHz) and utilized for the microwave solvothermal treatment in the synthesis of CQDs, as well as for the measurements of temperature, inner pressure, and microwave output power. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR: 500 MHz; JEOL)) and a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR: FT/IR-4600; JASCO)) were both utilized in the characterization of the synthesized carbon precursor (9,10-dinitroanthracene). FT-IR was also utilized for the determination of whatever functionalities were present on the surface of the CQDs. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed on a V-7300 JASCO spectrophotometer, while the 2D-excitation/emission analysis was performed on an FP-8600 JASCO spectrofluorimeter; photoluminescence (PL) and quantum yield (Φ) measurements were done on an FP-8600 JASCO apparatus. Structural properties of the CQDs were ascertained by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrometry (as above), by X-ray diffraction {XRD; RINT 2100, X-ray irradiation: CuKα (Rigaku Co.)} and by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI VersaProbe II, ΦULVAC-PHI Inc.). Particle size distributions of the CQDs were ascertained by transmission electron microscopy {TEM: mitH-7650, Accelerating voltage: 120 kV (Hitachi High-Technologies)} and by dynamic light scattering (DLS; Zetasizer nano ZS, Malvern). Relative dielectric loss measurements were done on a network analyzer apparatus (E5071C, probe: 85070−20037, Keysight technology Co. Ltd.).

The photoemission spectrum of Rhodamine B dye (range covered, 200–450 nm) recorded with illumination from a halogen lamp (range, 350–850 nm) was used as a reference in measurements of the photoluminescence of CQDs. In every PL measurement, the PL of the dispersing solvent (ethanol) was used as the baseline. In all cases, a 1-mm quartz cuvette was used to record the photoluminescence so as to avoid any possible concentration quenching. The quantum yields were determined by an absolute method using an apparatus equipped with an integrating sphere such that the ratio of the number of photons absorbed to the number of photons emitted by the CQDs was automatically calculated using the integrated software.

Determination of the size and size distributions of the quantum dots was achieved by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique using a He-Ne gas laser (maximum output power, 4 mW; beam diameter, 0.63 mm (1 e–²); beam divergence, 1.5 mrad; beam wavelength, 632.8 nm).

For XPS measurements, the pass energy and EV step were set as 58.70 eV and 0.125 eV, respectively. In the XPS spectral analysis, all XPS peaks were computer-fitted to 80 % Gaussian and 20 % Lorentzian mixed functions (Voigt functions), after performing a Shirley background subtraction. In the fitting procedure, all the fitted peaks are referred to a previous report [22]; the FWHM values were fixed at a maximum limit of 1.0 eV for all peaks.

Carbon quantum dots are typically synthesized using mostly carbon-based raw materials such as biomass (e.g., cellulose and wood scraps [23] and chemical products. Although biomass is eco-friendly from a Green Chemistry perspective, it suffers in quality maintenance and productivity of CQDs in industrial applications. Accordingly, we focused on low-cost, accessible, and (nearly) non-toxic products as the carbon precursors (e.g., polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons; PAHs) for the microwave-assisted solvothermal synthesis of CQDs because of their robust π-π stacking between the PAH molecules and their good thermal stability [24]. In this regard, compared to other well-known PAHs, anthracene was selected as the initial carbon precursor because of its highly crystalline nature [25].

Typically, aromatic compounds emit in the range 370–440 nm – for instance, anthracene emits strongly at 400–415 nm [26] – so that the emitted purple light from the samples may have originated from the unreacted carbon precursor, 9,10-dinitroanthracene, and/or from intermediates prior to the formation of the CQDs. Other emission origins may alo have involved species that were highly soluble in water and water-soluble organic solvents (ethanol and acetone) but not in hexane. This infers that the surfaces of the as-synthesized CQDs are polar and hydrophilic, and as such the CDQs were completely dispersed in aqueous media. There were two other types of CQDs in other water-soluble organic solvents: (i) one type emitted in the blue spectral region, while (ii) the other emitted in the yellow region. Except for methanol (as evidenced in Fig. 2a), ethanol and ethylene glycol played a significant role in the synthesis of yellow luminescent CQDs that displayed quantum yields of 36.3 % (ethanol) and 48.9 % (ethylene glycol) in the long-wavelength region relative to other solvents. It is not unlikely that differences in boiling points of the solvents may be responsible for the significant differences in the internal pressure during the reactions: < 5 atm for ethylene glycol and < 30 atm for ethanol (see Fig. S3 in Supplementary Information).

The resulting yellow luminescent CQDs from the synthesis in ethylene glycol were subsequently dissolved in various solvents (water, methanol, ethanol, 2-propanol, butanol, acetone, and hexane) for the purification stages, samples from which emitted light in three color ranges (purple, blue and yellow) when exposed to UV radiation as illustrated in Fig. 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic inferences on solvent-dependent photophysical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The above results indicate that the photoluminescence is strongly dependent on the nature of the solvent used in the synthesis of the CQDs and most importantly on the photophysical properties (e.g., luminescence quantum yields and emission wavelength) that could be viewed as a solvatochromism effect typically observed in organic dyes {see ref. [27] and references therein}. In this regard, several studies [27–30] have addressed some of the photophysical properties of carbon dots and their related applications such as, among others, (i) their practical use in LEDs [27], (ii) in biolabeling and in vitro and in vivo bioimaging, and as high-performing optical nanoprobes to replace various biolabeling and contrasting agents [28], and (iii) as optical electronic noses for real-time detection of alcohol vapors and volatile organic compounds [30].

Wu and coworkers [27] produced organic-soluble CQDs (o-CQDs) with a high yield of ∼90 % using a facile solvent-engineered treatment of 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as both a nitrogen and a carbon source, and investigated their optical properties in nonpolar and polar solvents (e.g., toluene, ethyl acetate (EA), acetone, and dimethyl formamide (DMF)). They observed a pronounced solvent effect in that their strong absorption bands could be tuned over the whole visible region (400–750 nm) by changing the solvent, in addition to which the CQDs emitted bright, excitation-independent, and long-wavelength fluorescence (orange to red) in those solvents. While the o-CQDs exhibited similar absorption bands in the high-energy region (200–350 nm), in the lower energy region (350–800 nm), the absorption spectra of diverse o-CQDs solutions exhibited different absorption edges at 575 nm (toluene), 620 nm (EA), 630 nm (acetone) and 715 nm (DMF), notable redshifts with increasing solvent polarity {toluene (2.4) < EA (4.3) < acetone (4.4) < DMF (6.4)}. The corresponding photoluminescence diplayed similar red shifts occurring at 606 nm, 625 nm, 637 nm and 662 nm. The emission quantum yields in the different solvents increased {9.47 % (DMF), 35.01 % (acetone), 50.61 % (EA) and 65.93 % (toluene)} with a decrease of the solvent’s polarity, which the authors [27] attributed to preferable solubility of the o-CQDs in less polar solvents; the solvent-dependent photoluminescence was regarded as a dipole-induced change in the electronic state of the o-CQD surface.

For their part, Pal and coworkers [28] examined the effect of solvent on the fluorescent behavior of as-synthesized carbon dots (CDs) and passivated carbon dots (PCDs; with branched polyethyleneimine) dissolved in water, methanol, DMF, and DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide). Both carbon dots in water displayed promising fluorescent quantum yields owing to their high solubility in this solvent, although PCDs showed maximal fluorescence in DMF solvent that was attributed to the presence of the amide group on the surface of the carbon as verified by FT-IR [28]. Structural and optical property characterizations by Chen et al. [29] of their carbon dots revealed concentration-dependent photoluminescent properties ascribed to the existence of multi-emissive centers in the carbon dots from core states, edge states and surface states on the basis of the preservation effect and inductive effect of the particular solvent (e.g., the polar aprotic DMF solvent). The optimal emissive center of the carbon dot sample remained unchanged at 585 nm in both DMF and water under illumination at 550 nm and 490 nm, respectively, inferring that the origin of the photoluminescence was the same in both solvents. However, there was a difference of ca. 50 nm in the emission wavelengths of the carbon dots in DMF and water when the excitation wavelength was 550 nm for both solvents; the integrated emission intensity in DMF was nearly 24 times greater than in water [29]. Carbon dots (4–5 nm) synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal/calcination method from rice husk exhibited a highly photostable blue emission, albeit with a quantum yield of only 3 % [30]. Nonetheless, such carbon dots proved capable of detecting alcohol vapors at room temperature, and of distinguishing between methanol, ethanol, and several volatile organic compounds when used as the sensing layer in an optical electronic nose system [30].

In our present study, the data illustrated in Fig. 2a infer that ethylene glycol was likely responsible for the relatively high emission quantum yields of the CQDs (Φ = 48.9 %) and for process safety. Some time ago, Hu and coworkers [31] noted that surface functionalization was very important for the luminescence of CQDs as they could not emit light without surface functionalization, not least of which are such environmental conditions as pH and polarity of the solutions that not only influence the optical properties but also the luminescence wavelength [32]. Earlier studies [33,34] also noted that luminescence from carbon quantum dots originates from defect states with the oxygenous functional groups. In this regard, Sun et al. [32] recently defined what species played a dominating role in the photoluminescence emission from the CDQs using electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. They discovered that carboxyl groups (citric acid was a component in their synthesis of the CQDs) played a non-insignificant role in their observed green emission, which they attributed to the presence of surface energy traps, as reduction of the oxygen-containing surface groups led to a blue shift of the fluorescence, whereas oxidation of the surface groups led to a red shift of the photoluminescence [32]. Taking a clue from semiconductor physics, carbon quantum dots also possess an electronic structure that involves both HOMO (valence band; VB) and LUMO (conduction band; CB) bands (see Scheme 1 ). Accordingly, UV/Vis irradiation at the absorption edge generates the photocarriers (electrons and holes) that subsequent to relaxation could be directly trapped into their corresponding surface states as shown for the electrons.

The photogenerated carriers can relax or be trapped directly into the surface states from which a broad distribution of transitions could dominate separately under different excitations that would thus impart an excitation-dependent behavior of the CQDs. Moreover, doping and functionalization can vary the optical properties of the CQDs such as the quantum yield and the photoluminescence wavelength [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the synthesis of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Ethylene glycol is a well-known good absorber of microwave radiation and so it contributes to a rapid and energy-efficient microwave-assisted synthesis of the CQDs. A comparison of the microwave-assisted solvothermal (MWS) method with the conventional autoclave solvothermal (CAS) protocol allows for exploring the peculiar characteristics of the microwave method. In this regard, ethylene glycol enhanced the rapid microwave heating (optimal) with the heating rate being 148 °C min–1 in contrast to the 1.54 °C min–1 for the CAS method, a near 100–fold faster heating rate for the MWS protocol. As a consequence, the total reaction time was 3.25 h for the microwave method versus 5.0 h for the autoclave method (Fig. S4a). With regard to expended energy and efficiency, the output power consumed was 4.44 kW h for the MWS versus 344 kW h for the CAS method, highlighting a near 80-fold lower energy consumption in the former (Fig. S4b). Surprisingly, the luminescence quantum yield (Φ = 22.2 %) of the MWS-synthesized CQDs was 2-fold smaller than the luminescence quantum yield (Φ = 48.9 %) from the CAS-synthesized CQDs. Consequently, we deduce that the MWS method may not have been a suitable methodology for the synthesis of highly luminescent CQDs. Accordingly, the MWS experiments were terminated and the samples left on a desk. To our surprise, however, when we inadvertently examined those samples after a few days later, we discovered – albeit serendipitously – a big difference in the photoluminescence stemming from the samples. The CAS-synthesized CQDs that initially displayed a relatively higher emission quantum yield witnessed a fairly rapid decrease of their luminescence after only 1 day and no longer emitted after 4 days (Fig. 2b). By contrast, the photoluminescence from the MWS-synthesized CQDs was stable for ca. 4 days, after which it no longer photoluminesced on day 5 (Fig. 2b; see also Fig. S5). The decreased photoluminescence is likely the result either of the deterioration of the CQDs and/or to a self-quenching effect resulting from aggregation of the PEG-free nanoparticulates as occurs in dyes [35–37].

Besides exploring the characteristics of the microwave method, the stability of the as-synthesized CQDs was also an important aspect of our investigations. Germane to this, Park et al. [38] noted that the instability of quantum dots results mostly from the instability of the particle surface. Accordingly, it was relevant next to examine how the surface of the CQDs could be protected while the CQDs were monodispersed. The stability of the luminescence from CQDs depends on modifying the surface with such polymers as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [39], polyethyleneimine (PEI) [5,40] and poly(ethylenimide)-co-poly(ethyleneglycol)-copoly(ethylenimide) (PPEI) [39], among others [41]. For example, Sun and coworkers [39] functionalized the surface of non-luminescent CQDs with PEG-1500 (mean molar weight, 1500) that resulted in bright surface-emitting CQDs. It appears then that the polymer shell covering the particles played a significant role, not only on the stability of the photoluminescence but also in the significant increase of the photoluminescence quantum yields of the CQDs. Following their observations, polyethylene glycol (PEG) was added to the carbon precursor/ethylene glycol mixed solution during the synthesis of PEG-functionalized CQDs. This resulted in highly stable luminescent CQDs; in fact, the photoluminescence was stable even a week later as determined by the measured quantum yields (Fig. 2b and Table 1 ). Surprisingly, the MWS-synthesized CQDs with added PEG displayed a significantly higher luminescence quantum yield (Φ = 61.1 %) relative to the quantum yield of the CAS-synthesized CQDs (Φ = 42.3 %). Optimization of the types, amounts, and degrees of polymerization of highly photoluminescent CQDs (Φs > 50 %) has received but limited attention.

In the MWS method, increasing the amount of PEG led to an increase in the emission quantum yield (Table 1). For instance, increasing the amount of PEG-6000 from 0.15 to 0.60 g increased the quantum yield from 39.2 % to 61.1 %. By contrast, increasing the degree of polymerization of PEG revealed no correlation with changes in quantum yield. For example, though the emission quantum yield from CQDs synthesized in the presence of 0.15 g of PEG-600 was 42.9 %, the luminescence quantum yield in the as-synthesized CQDs with 0.15 g of PEG-6000 was slightly lower (Φ = 39.2 %). In addition, the quantum yields depended on the added amount of PEG-6000 as illustrated in Fig. 2c, which shows that Φ increases with increased amount of PEG-6000 for the MWS-synthesized CQDs, yet there was a slight decrease (if any) of the quantum yields for the CAS-synthesized CQDs.

The origin of the difference in the quantum yields of CQDs synthesized by the same heating source is supported by a previously reported study by Chen and coworkers [42], who highlighted the decomposition of PEG under high temperature conditions (over 100 °C) together with the importance of the quantity (but not the degree of polymerization) of the PEG added to the reaction vessel. To explain the difference in the quantum yields observed between the heating sources (MWS versus CAS) we also analyzed what crucial and significant factors support this photoluminescence difference from the viewpoint of chemical and morphological differences of the as-synthesized CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differences in the chemical structures of PEG-free CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Differences observed between the MWS-synthesized CQDs and the CAS-synthesized CQDs are rather enigmatic, in that such differences may originate from differences in their respective chemical structures. Consequently, these were examined by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectrometry, and by FT-IR, XPS and XRD techniques (see also Figs. S6 and S7). At first, we compared the structures of PEG-free CQDs prepared by both MWS and CAS methods. The 1H-NMR spectra showed that the PEG-free CQDs consist of strong singlet peaks at 1.76, 3.51, 3.56, 3.78 and 8.30 ppm that we ascribe to the CCH2 C, CH2 OCCQD , CH2 OCH2 , OCH3, and CCQD H functions, respectively, regardless of the difference in the two heating sources (Fig. 4 a-i and Table S1a-i). Similar observations were reported in comparable studies on polyaromatic hydrocarbons [43,44], graphene oxide (GO) [43] and CQDs [44]. The 13C-NMR spectra revealed a strong peak at 69.7 ppm attributed to an ether-type linkage (CH2 OCH2 ; see Fig. 4b-i).

Apart from the CH bending mode at 1317–1384 cm–1 and the aromatic CCstretching mode at 1385–1580 cm–1, the FT-IR spectra highlight the OH/COOH (2500–3600 cm–1) and CO (1055–1101 cm–1) functions on the surface of the PEG-free CQDs (Fig. 3c-i), which contributed to a strong dispersity in aqueous media. XRD results displayed in Fig. 3d-i reveal that PEG-free CQDs by the MWS and the CAS treatments are amorphous.

There are no apparent chemical structural differences in CQDs prepared in the absence of PEG with regard to surface structure and crystallinity. Nevertheless, an XPS analysis of the core chemical structure of PEG-free CAS-synthesized CQDs reveals a greater number of lattice O atoms than the MWS-synthesized CQDs, which is likely due to the presence of −COOH moieties on the surface of the CAS-synthesized CQDs (Fig. S7 and Tables S1a and S1b, S2). On the other hand, XRD results of the MWS-synthesized PEG-free CQDs showed a greater number of amorphous sp 2 carbon-carbon bonds in the lattice. Thus far, the results infer a lattice structure of the CQDs core that contains a bond between the CCQD carbon and the ethylene glycol oxygen, OEG, which may be a key factor in the observed strong photoluminescence intensity of PEG-free CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differences in the chemical structures of CQDs prepared in the presence of PEG
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs surface-functionalized with 0.60 g of PEG-6000 revealed some chemical structural differences as established by 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR analyses: the singlet peaks at 3.51 ppm (1H-NMR) and 62.6 ppm (13C-NMR) were observed in the MWS-synthesized CQDs; however, no such peaks occurred in CAS-synthesized CQDs (Fig. 4a-ii and b-ii), except for the peaks observed for the PEG-free CQDs. These NMR singlets represent the function [45] –CCQD–OPEG−CH2–, which infers a microwave-induced dehydration/condensation reaction between the surface of the CQDs and the PEG-6000.

FT-IR spectra show differences at 3142.4 cm–1 (secondary NH vibration), 1094.4 cm–1 (NH vibration), 760.8 cm−1 (NH wagging) and at 663.4 cm–1 (NH wagging), which highlight the presence of an NH2 function on the surface or else in the core of the CAS-synthesized CQDs, but not in the MWS-synthesized CQDs (Fig. 4c-i and 4c-ii) [46]. A difference in heating sources likely brought about changes in the chemical structure and in the crystallinity of PEG-functionalized CQDs. XRD diffraction of the MWS-synthesized CQDs revealed a strong graphene oxide (GO; 002) crystalline peak at 11.14° (Fig. 4d-ii) on addition of PEG-6000 (0.60 g), in accord with a study on the GO synthesis [47]. XRD data also indicate a correlation between the crystallinity of CQDs and the increase of the photoluminescence quantum yield for the MWS-synthesized CQDs, in accord with an earlier study by Zheng et al. [48] on the correlation between crystallinity and luminescence.

A second possibility to clarify the obvious differences in photoluminescence is the involvement of the NH2 moiety on the CAS-synthesized CQDs surface, in that it may induce energy relaxation between the conduction band of the CQDs and a N-based intragap level as occurs in graphene quantum dots [49], thereby stabilizing the photoluminescence regardless of PEG functionalization. The XPS data (Table S1, Fig. S7) reveal the presence of 1.71 % of N atoms in the CAS-synthesized CQDs originating from a possible CNH2 bond in accord with the CNH2 moiety seen in the FT-IR spectra. By contrast, the MWS-synthesized CQDs showed no such N atoms. High-resolution XPS C 1s spectra, which highlight 19.17 area % of the strong COC moiety peak at 286.55 eV are in accord with the 1H-NMR spectral data; 58.4 % of the sp2 carbon bond peak was observed in MWS-synthesized CQDs (Fig. 4e-i, Table S1c-i). By comparison, the XPS C 1s spectra confirm that the CAS-synthesized CQDs contain 15.38 area % of a weak peak due to a COC moiety and 5.79 area % of a peak ascribed to a CNH2 function (Fig. 4f-i, Table S1d-i). Considerations of the ratio of COC against sp2 carbon moieties suggest that the MWS treatment promoted the bonding between the PEG and the surface of the CQDs (1.25 times greater) than is otherwise the case of CAS-synthesized CQDs.

XPS C 1s spectral results also accord with the XPS O 1s results: that is, the MWS-synthesized CQDs highlight a 1.13-fold greater number of CCQD O functions over the CAS-synthesized CQDs in terms of the ratio of aromatic CO peak area % (Fig. 4f-i and f-ii, Table S1c-ii and S1d-ii). The latter infers a third possibility in explaining the dramatic enhancement of the photoluminescence from the MWS-synthesized CQDs, the structure of which is typical of graphene oxide (GO) [50]. Analyses of the chemical structure and crystallinity of CQDs demonstrate that the observed differences in the photoluminescence of PEG-functionalized CQDs correlate strongly with the aromatic COPEG bond and GO-like crystallinity resulting from the two different heating sources.

Differences in nano-shaped CQDs were also examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) techniques, which revealed that MWS heating yielded highly luminescent, monodispersed and spherically shaped CQDs (2–9 nm) in the presence of 0.60 g of PEG-6000, whilst CAS heating produced carbon-based materials of various sizes and shapes (30–100 nm; spheres, triangles, and cylinders, among others; see Fig. 5 b). No difference between the MWS and CAS treatments was observed in PEG-free CQDs from the morphological viewpoint (Fig. 5a). Consequently, we infer that the difference between the MWS and CAS protocols caused the conspicuous dissimilarity in morphology, which may be at the origin of the PEG-dependent luminescence features of the CQDs.

Yuan and coworkers [7] showed that highly bright CQDs displayed a narrow size distribution (around 4.0 nm). Likewise, Yeh et al. [21] highlighted the existence of a quantum effect in GO-type yellow luminescent 5.4-nm CQDs, which accords with the crystallinity and particle size of PEG-functionalized MWS-synthesized CQDs. On the other hand, the CAS-assisted PEG-functionalization afforded amorphous yellow luminescent CQDs that were mostly carbon-based nanomaterials of variable shapes and large size distribution. Theoretically, the latter CQDs exhibited no photoluminescence in the yellow spectral region [21]. Earlier reports also inferred that differences in sizes and shapes of the narrowly-distributed CQDs produced by MWS heating and the variable-sized carbon-based materials formed by CAS heating may be at the origin of the apparent differences in photoluminescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of microwave heating and how microwaves influence CQD performance
ce:section.ce:para: The difference in photoluminescence resulting from structural and morphological differences was observed whenever PEG-6000 (0.60 g) was added to the reaction. This suggests that microwaves interacted beneficially with this surface-protective agent, which resulted in an apparent morphological advantage. This was further investigated by heating a precursor-free ethylene glycol-PEG mixed solution with the same microwave power input as an ethylene glycol-PEG mixed solution containing the carbon precursor; results are displayed in Fig. 6 a. The inner temperatures increased rapidly to 210 °C for solutions with and without the precursor present, that later decreased to 55 °C after ca. 1 h when no precursor was present (Fig. 6b). This demonstrates an inordinate temperature anomaly (ΔT =155 °C) generated in the microphase, which implies that the carbon precursor and/or some intermediate species strongly absorbed the microwave energy that led to a homogeneous production of CQDs.

The selectivity of MWS heating was also investigated to determine explicitly the relative dielectric losses of (i) ethylene glycol (solvent), (ii) the mixed ethylene glycol-PEG solution containing no carbon precursor, and (iii) the mixed ethylene glycol-PEG solution containing the carbon precursor (Fig. 6c). The decrease of the relative dielectric losses for conditions (i) and (ii) dropped from 14.7 to 8.9, while the relative dielectric loss (ε r″ = 14–15) for solution (iii) remained essentially constant, regardless of the temperature increase; this indicates that carbon-based species strongly absorbed the microwave radiation insofar as carbon-based nanomaterials (e.g., carbon nanotubes) tend to be excellent microwave absorbers [51,52]. It is reasonable to infer, therefore, that the as-synthesized CQDs are the key factor that confirms the advantage of MWS heating.

The sizes and shapes of the carbon-based species were determined using a camera attached to the microwave apparatus to observe events occurring in-situ during the reaction. Fig. 6d highlights the color changes (reddish-orange to dark red) of the solution after 60 min of MWS heating. The solution exposed for 10 and 60 min to MWS heating was subsequently examined for an ex-situ morphological inspection by TEM. Fig. 6e displays aggregates of nanoparticles that exhibited very low photoluminescence quantum yields, indicating that such nanoparticles are different from the CQDs that form on heating for 3 h. Fig. 6e also reveals relatively large particles with sizes 300–350 nm obtained after heating for 60 min, suggesting that carbon precursors are somehow polymerized into sub-bulky carbons first, followed by their pyrolysis into round-shaped nano-CQDs.

Selective microwave-heated sub-bulky carbons could, in principle, show extreme increases of the inner temperatures over their surrounding environment that consisted of NaOH, ethylene glycol and PEG. However, determination of temperatures of selectively microwave-heated nanoparticles dispersed in a solvent remains a difficult task, although temperature measurements of microwave-heated nanoparticles (size: ca. 10 nm or more; metals or metal oxides) were previously carried out by Raman spectroscopy [53].

Since the first report on the reaction mechanism of CQDs synthesized from organic acids and bases by Song and coworkers in 2015 [54], synthetic mechanisms into the formation of CQDs from aromatic carbon precursors have yet to be described [55]. A plausible mechanistic reaction model may be inferred from comparisons made in this study. In the first instance, nitration of anthracene performed in aqueous HNO3 media under reflux conditions yielded 9,10-dinitroanthracene, following which denitration via a substitution reaction in alkaline media (OH; under reflux conditions) gave 9,10-dihydroxyanthracene. Subsequent MWS-assisted or CAS-assisted dehydration and condensation (Fig. 7 a) yielded some polymer that ultimately turned into a sub-bulky carbon-based substrate (Fig. 7b). Pyrolysis of the latter and further functionalization with PEG (Fig. 7b) via microwave selective heating affected positively only the homogenous pyrolysis of the sub-bulky carbon-based substrate that finally led to highly luminescent PEG-functionalized CQDs. By contrast, unlike MWS heating, CAS heating was non-selective so that re-polymerization of the sub-bulky/cluster carbon and/or predominant pyrolysis of PEG ultimately generated carbon-based materials of variable sizes and shapes (Fig. 7c).

The MWS-driven synthetic method of producing carbon quantum dots reported herein successfully yielded highly yellow luminescent and monodispersed CQDs whose surfaces were coordinated to PEG. Moreover, MWS heating contributed to an energy-efficient synthesis of monodispersed CQDs with lower power consumption (nearly 80-fold lower) and shorter reaction times (1.5-fold) at a remarkable rapid heating rate compared to the CAS-heating methodology. The selective heating feature of the microwaves is also of significance in leading to a uniform pyrolysis of carbon-based species that resulted in high-quality CQDs from the viewpoint of chemical structure, crystallinity, monodispersity, and photoluminescence. Accordingly, the use of MWS heating should be investigated further toward the selective synthesis and functionalization of microwave-heated QD and CQD nano-/micro-materials heretofore produced by different methodologies.
